summary: Joel Spolsky notes that Fog Creek's internal FogBugz database has passed 500,000 case numbers. He quickly clarifies that the vast majority are spam, faxes, customer emails, to-do items, and other non-bug entries. The actual breakdown reveals only 66,443 real cases, 7,303 fixed bugs, and 909 implemented features. The post serves as a lighthearted showcase of how FogBugz is used as an all-purpose tracking system, not just a bug tracker.
key_points: FogBugz hit 500,000 case numbers, but the vast majority are spam that gets auto-assigned a case number before deletion
The tool is used for far more than bugs: faxes, customer email, to-do lists, job applications, phone messages, sysadmin notifications, and crash reports
Out of 500,000 cases, only 66,443 are real cases, with 7,303 actual bugs fixed and 909 features implemented
815 bugs could not be reproduced — a wry closing detail that any developer will recognize
money_quotes: Last night, at some point, we passed case number 500,000 in the Fog Creek in-house FogBugz database.
We actually have 66,443 real cases and 489,022 historical events.
We have replied to 33,692 customer emails using FogBugz, fixed 7,303 bugs, and implemented 909 features.
Incoming spam gets assigned a case number, even though it usually lands in a spam folder and gets deleted after seven days, so most of those cases are spam.
key_insight: A good issue tracker becomes the central nervous system of a software company, handling far more than just bugs — and the raw case count tells you almost nothing about actual engineering work.
